persistent_read_probe.note: Single backend, NO reconnect, 60 colbert_search calls on the PERSISTENT vec_colbert_ops index. RSS plateaus at ~360 MB by call 10 and is flat thereafter (28.7 KB/call from call 10->60). The F-1 backend-cache path leaked ~28000 KB/call (rebuilt the token index + thread-local slot->doc map every call), forcing the SciFact harness to reconnect every 40 queries. The F-2 persistent path does NOT rebuild per call: this flat plateau is the direct confirmation that Arm A reads the persistent relfile-backed index (warm from the shared cache), not a per-call rebuild. The reconnect-every-40 guard was kept in the sweep as belt-and-suspenders but is no longer needed.

arch_notes: x86_64 AVX2 (turbovec SIMD kernels active; latency meaningful). CPU torch only for embedding. pg16 shared_buffers=256MB, maintenance_work_mem=512MB. pg_turbovec v1.17.0; persistent vec_colbert_ops (F-2 SHIPPED) index exercised.
query_path: Arm A = turbovec.colbert_search auto-routing to the PERSISTENT vec_colbert_ops index (F-2), confirmed by find_colbert_index + the flat-RSS probe. This is NOT the F-1 backend-cache rebuild path.
memory_safety: Embedding ran under systemd-run --user --scope MemoryMax=12G MemorySwapMax=0 (cgroup cap verified to KILL a 500MB-over-200M-cap probe first). Batched (32 docs), gc.collect() between batches, incremental .npy writes. Sweep client kept the reconnect-every-40 guard (though the persistent path no longer leaks). MemAvailable never dropped below ~18.6 GiB (6 GiB floor); the postmaster was never at risk; no kill -9; no OOM.
corpus_character: NFCorpus (BeIR/nfcorpus): medical/nutrition domain, OUT-OF-DOMAIN vs SciFact's scientific text, entity-heavier queries. 3633 docs, 323 test queries, 12334 graded qrels (mean 38.2 judgments/query, scores 1-2). Note: with median 16 relevant docs/query, Recall@10 is structurally capped well below 1.0 (can retrieve at most 10), so nDCG@10 is the primary metric; Recall@10 deltas are still directionally meaningful.

verdict.question: Does the SciFact ColBERT recall gain (qualified GO) REPLICATE on NFCorpus (2nd, out-of-domain, entity-heavier corpus)?
verdict.answer: CONFIRM. The recall/nDCG gain replicates with the SAME shape (largest at low candidate budget, shrinking as the pooled baseline catches up at high candidate_n).

value_operating_point.comment: NFCorpus +0.044 nDCG / +0.037 Recall vs SciFact +0.062 nDCG / +0.088 Recall. Smaller absolute deltas, same SIGN and same mechanism. Recall delta is compressed by NFCorpus's many-relevant-docs-per-query structure (Recall@10 caps far below 1). The nDCG gain is clearly present and significant.

low_candidate_budget.comment: THE F-2 THESIS HOLDS. At candidate_n=128 the delta GROWS to +0.065 nDCG / +0.048 Recall (vs +0.044/+0.037 at cand=256) because the pooled baseline collapses (Arm B nDCG 0.220, its relevant docs fall outside the pooled top-128) while colbert_search holds at 0.285 via best-single-token retrieval. SciFact showed the identical collapse (+0.103/+0.135). The gain is concentrated exactly where F-2's value proposition lives: high recall at low candidate budget.

high_candidate_budget.comment: At candidate_n=512 the baseline pulls most relevant docs in (Arm B nDCG 0.261) and the delta shrinks to +0.022 nDCG / +0.018 Recall — same convergence as SciFact (+0.014 at cand=512). Confirms the gain is a low-budget recall effect, not a uniform win.

quantization.comment: Signal INTACT under quantization, replicating SciFact. At matched (ptk=64,cand=256): 2-bit nDCG 0.2830 vs 4-bit 0.2829 (delta 0.0001). At (ptk=128,cand=512): 2-bit 0.2828 == 4-bit 0.2828. The plan's single biggest risk (2-4 bit destroys the token signal) did NOT materialise on NFCorpus either. 2-bit is sufficient (43.3 MB index vs 79.5 MB at 4-bit).

ceiling.comment: Exact brute-force MaxSim ceiling: nDCG 0.2823 / Recall 0.1428. Arm A REACHES the ceiling (e.g. cand=256: 0.2829 nDCG / 0.1427 Recall ~= ceiling; cand=128 even edges to 0.2846 by a top-128 reordering). A single-query top-10 smoke check gave 10/10 overlap with brute force at ptk=128/cand=512. The persistent index serves CORRECTLY.

persistent_index.comment: Persistent vec_colbert_ops index (F-2, wire v5, kind=colbert) built cleanly in ~42-52s for 561,120 token slots (lists=749 ~= sqrt(n_tokens)), 43.3 MB at 2-bit / 79.5 MB at 4-bit on disk. No OOM during build (memory steady ~19 GiB). Arm A read the persistent index (flat-RSS probe + ORDER BY rejected = kind=colbert v5). The F-1 ~28 MB/call backend leak is GONE on this path.

verdict.overall: CONFIRMED. The SciFact recall gain REPLICATES on NFCorpus, a second, out-of-domain, entity-heavier corpus. Same sign, same mechanism, same shape (delta largest at low candidate_n: +0.065 nDCG at cand=128, shrinking to +0.022 at cand=512). Absolute deltas are ~30% smaller than SciFact's, partly because NFCorpus's dense graded qrels compress Recall@10, but the nDCG@10 advantage is unambiguous and positive at every config. Quantization leaves the signal intact (2-bit == 4-bit). The persistent F-2 index built cleanly, served correctly (reaches the exact ceiling), and eliminated the F-1 per-call rebuild/leak. The qualified GO from corpus #1 is UPGRADED to an established cross-domain recall win. The win is specifically 'high recall at low candidate budget', exactly F-2's value proposition, and it holds out-of-domain.
